refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and link health monitoring
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic route optimization
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular link + secondary satellite/mesh backup with intelligent switching
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid approach—primary cellular link with secondary satellite/mesh backup, intelligent link health monitoring, and automatic failover to achieve ≥0.999 availability. Includes continuous telemetry of link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) and deterministic safe-state transition on primary link loss.

potential_sources: DP-D couples link availability to network infrastructure (cellular + satellite providers); availability depends on external provider SLAs and geographic coverage.
DP-D couples safe-state transition logic to link loss detection; requires tight integration with Safety Services for deterministic failsafe behavior.
DP-D couples telemetry bandwidth to link quality monitoring; increased monitoring overhead may reduce available bandwidth for operational telemetry.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable because they are explicit and manageable: (1) external provider SLAs are contractual and measurable; (2) safe-state logic is owned by Safety Services; (3) telemetry overhead is quantifiable and can be tuned. No hidden or circular dependencies detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cellular and satellite providers can deliver ≥99.9% availability in target operational areas.
ASSUMPTION: Failover switching can be executed in <100 ms without data loss.
ASSUMPTION: Secondary link (satellite/mesh) has sufficient bandwidth for critical telemetry and commands.
RISK: Cellular coverage gaps in remote worksites may violate availability target; mitigation: pre-site survey and mesh backup.
RISK: Satellite latency (>500 ms) may degrade operator experience; mitigation: operator training and UI feedback on link mode.
RISK: Dual-link cost and complexity increase system cost and maintenance burden; mitigation: cost-benefit analysis against safety criticality.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) primary link technology (e.g., LTE/5G), (2) secondary link technology (e.g., Iridium satellite or LoRa mesh), (3) failover decision criteria (e.g., packet loss >5% for >2 seconds triggers switch), (4) link health telemetry schema, (5) safe-state definition and transition time budget.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-link communication stack, link health monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry/tasking message routing. Interfaces with Vehicle for command execution and Safety Services for safe-state coordination. Does NOT own the physical network infrastructure (cellular/satellite) or the Vehicle's propulsion/control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, etc.) from tele-operator console; routed through primary or secondary link based on link health.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) to tele-operator console and monitoring systems; includes link health status.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safe-state transition signals from Safety Services on link loss; transmits link loss alerts to Safety Services for coordinated failsafe behavior.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying dual-link infrastructure (cellular + satellite); Teleoperation module manages link selection, health monitoring, and failover without owning physical network hardware.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover, and telemetry routing. High containment: all link management, health monitoring, and message routing are localized. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert coordination with Safety Services (expected and manageable).

Safety Services should NOT own link availability; it owns safe-state definition and transition. If link logic were in Safety Services, it would diffuse across network, telemetry, and tasking concerns. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns propulsion and control; link availability is a system-level concern, not vehicle-specific. Placing link logic in Vehicle would couple communication infrastructure to vehicle hardware. Not recommended.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link availability could be allocated here, but it would lack clear ownership and interface boundaries. Better to allocate to Teleoperation (a subsystem) for clarity and testability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of link availability because: (1) it owns the communication stack and message routing; (2) link health monitoring is a communication concern, not a safety or vehicle concern; (3) failover logic is tightly coupled to telemetry/tasking message handling; (4) interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all natural to Teleoperation; (5) containment score 0.95 indicates minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module is not properly isolated from Vehicle control logic, link failover could inadvertently trigger unintended vehicle movements. MITIGATION: Enforce strict interface contracts; SafetyAlert is the only channel for link-loss-triggered safe-state transitions.

RISK: If Network Link abstraction is not well-defined, Teleoperation could become tightly coupled to specific cellular/satellite hardware. MITIGATION: Define Network Link as a clean abstraction layer; Teleoperation depends on Network Link interface, not on hardware details.

RISK: Telemetry bandwidth overhead from link health monitoring could reduce operational telemetry capacity. MITIGATION: Quantify overhead; allocate separate telemetry channel for link metrics if needed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Formally define the Network Link abstraction interface (e.g., link_status(), switch_to_secondary(), get_link_metrics()) to ensure Teleoperation does not depend on cellular/satellite implementation details. Document the SafetyAlert contract for link-loss coordination.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-link with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and feasible. Coupling analysis shows no hidden dependencies. Assumptions and risks are explicit and manageable. DP is ready for IM assignment.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must maintain clean separation from Vehicle control; SafetyAlert is the only coordination channel.
Network Link abstraction must be enforced to prevent tight coupling to cellular/satellite hardware.
Telemetry bandwidth overhead must be monitored and controlled.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. All link management, failover, and telemetry routing are localized. Only expected diffusion is SafetyAlert coordination with Safety Services (a safety-critical interface, properly managed). IM assignment is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) automated link state logging during evaluation period; (2) statistical analysis of availability; (3) acceptance criterion is quantitative (≥0.999). Teleoperation module can expose link_availability() metric for audit.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability; Safety Services owns safe-state transition; Vehicle owns command execution. Interfaces are well-defined (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No ambiguity.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify failover decision criteria (e.g., packet loss threshold, latency threshold, decision timeout).
Define Network Link abstraction interface formally (e.g., as an API contract or protocol specification).
Document SafetyAlert contract for link-loss coordination between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Allocate sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) to specific design elements (e.g., link loss detection to Teleoperation, safe-state transition to Safety Services).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs addressing link detection, safe-state transition, redundancy, and audit. DP-D is sound and well-coupled. Teleoperation is the correct IM owner with 0.95 containment. All gates pass. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail link detection logic, failover criteria, safe-state coordination, and audit mechanisms. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall support redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

assumptions: Cellular and satellite providers deliver ≥99.9% availability in target operational areas.
Failover switching can execute in <100 ms without data loss.
Secondary link has sufficient bandwidth for critical telemetry and commands.
Operator training and UI feedback mitigate satellite latency concerns.
Network Link abstraction can be cleanly separated from Teleoperation logic.
open_risks: Cellular coverage gaps in remote worksites may violate availability target; mitigation: pre-site survey and mesh backup.
Satellite latency (>500 ms) may degrade operator experience; mitigation: operator training and UI feedback.
Dual-link cost and complexity increase system cost; mitigation: cost-benefit analysis against safety criticality.
Telemetry bandwidth overhead from link health monitoring; mitigation: quantify and allocate separate channel if needed.
Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Vehicle control if SafetyAlert contract is not enforced; mitigation: formal interface definition.
evidence_needed: Cellular and satellite provider SLA documentation for target operational areas.
Cost-benefit analysis: dual-link cost vs. safety criticality and availability target.
Network Link abstraction specification (API contract or protocol).
SafetyAlert coordination protocol between Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Telemetry bandwidth overhead quantification and allocation plan.
